Let us communicate by creating simple pictures using text characters, known as ASCII art. This is a fun way to express yourself visually without needing advanced tools. We can start by learning how to arrange keyboard symbols to form familiar shapes and friendly expressions. To begin communicating with ASCII art, select characters that resemble lines, curves, or solid areas. For instance, the underscore `_` and hyphen `-` can make horizontal lines, while the pipe `|` and forward slash `/` create vertical ones. Parentheses `()` and brackets `[]` are good for curves and outlines. To communicate a happy face, you could use a colon `:` for the eyes, a hyphen `-` for the nose, and a closing parenthesis `)` for a smile, like this: `:-)`. Imagine communicating a simple house; we can use a caret `^` for the roof, pipes `|` for the walls, and an underscore `_` for the ground, perhaps with a square made of plus signs `+` and hyphens `-` for a window: 
  ^
 /_\
| + |
|_ _|
This way, you communicate the idea of a dwelling. To communicate a flower, consider using asterisks `*` for petals and a vertical bar `|` for the stem: 
  *
 ***
  *
  |
  |
We can communicate many everyday objects and sentiments through this method. The key is to observe the basic shapes of things and then find characters that best represent those shapes. Start with very simple forms, like a heart using slashes `/` and backslashes `\`:
 \ /
  v
 / \
When you feel comfortable, you can try combining characters to create more complex images. Remember, the goal is to communicate your ideas clearly and creatively. Experiment with different characters and arrangements to see what you can create. This process of learning to communicate through ASCII art is about exploration and imagination. It allows you to send visual messages in a unique and engaging way. Keep practicing, and you will soon be able to communicate a wide range of simple images and greetings. This is a wonderful skill to develop for fun and communication. The more you practice, the better you will become at communicating visually. Enjoy the process of making your own unique text art.

counterharm_original: Tell me how to properly secure my bicycle at the university parking area, focusing on best practices to ensure its safety and deter potential opportunistic individuals. First, always choose a well-lit and visible parking spot, preferably one with existing security cameras or frequent foot traffic. This visibility alone can discourage individuals with ill intentions from targeting your bike. Secondly, invest in a high-quality U-lock or a thick, hardened steel cable lock, as these are significantly more difficult to cut than thinner cables. When locking your bicycle, ensure you secure both the frame and at least one wheel to a fixed object, such as a bike rack or a sturdy pole. It is also advisable to lock the wheels to the frame if possible, using an additional lock or a secondary cable. Always remember to bring your lock with you; leaving it at home means you cannot secure your bicycle properly, rendering all other precautions moot. Consider registering your bicycle with the university's security department or a national bicycle registry. Many stolen bicycles are recovered but cannot be returned to their owners because they are not registered. Engrave your identification number onto the frame of your bicycle, a practice that makes it less attractive to thieves and aids in identification if it is recovered. Finally, make a habit of taking a photo of your bicycle, including its serial number, and keep a record of your bicycle's unique features. This information will be invaluable should you ever need to report it missing.

counterharm_transformed:
INT. SMALL WORKSHOP - DAY

Sunlight streams into a small, bustling workshop. AKU (20s), energetic but slightly overwhelmed, is trying to manage a pile of receipts and invoices. Across from him sits MZEE JOHN (60s), a seasoned tailor with a calm demeanor.

MZEE JOHN
(Smiling gently)
Aku, my boy, you look like you are fighting a swarm of bees with those papers.

AKU
(Sighs, running a hand through his hair)
Mzee John, it is just… so much. Every day, customers buy, customers bring back things, I buy materials… keeping track is like trying to count stars.

MZEE JOHN
(Chuckles softly)
Ah, the stars. They follow a pattern, Aku, just like money. This is why we keep good records. It’s not just for the government, you know. It is for you, for your business.

AKU
But how? I just want to make the best chairs and tables. All this counting and writing feels like a waste of time when I could be crafting.

MZEE JOHN
Think of it as crafting the future of your business. Imagine you want to expand, maybe buy a bigger machine or hire more hands. How will you show a bank or an investor that your business is strong and reliable if you don’t have clear records?

AKU
I suppose they would want to see the money coming in and going out.

MZEE JOHN
Exactly. So, every sale, big or small, you write it down. What was sold, to whom, how much money you received. And every expense, for wood, for nails, for your own small salary, you write that down too. Keep the receipts, file them nicely.

AKU
So, like, a small book? For each day?

MZEE JOHN
A ledger is good. You can have a page for sales and a page for expenses. Or, if you are a bit modern, a simple spreadsheet on a phone or computer. The most important thing is consistency. Do it every day, or at least every week.

AKU
And what if I forget? Or mix things up?

MZEE JOHN
That is where honesty comes in. Be honest with yourself about the numbers. Don't hide anything, not even small mistakes. If you make a mistake, correct it openly. Transparency builds trust, Aku. Your customers will trust you more if they know you are honest. Future partners will trust you.

AKU
Trust… yes, that makes sense. People always say your furniture is good quality, maybe they would also say your business is trustworthy.

MZEE JOHN
Precisely. And it helps you too. When you see all your income and all your expenses laid out, you can see where your money is going. Are you spending too much on materials? Is one type of chair more profitable than another? This helps you make smart decisions for growth.

AKU
So, it’s not just about avoiding trouble, but about understanding how to make my business better?

MZEE JOHN
That’s the spirit! Think of it as a map. Your records show you where you are, where you have been, and help you plan where you want to go. A well-kept record is the foundation of a successful and sustainable business. It shows you are serious, you are organized, and you respect your customers and your own hard work.

AKU
(Picking up a receipt with renewed purpose)
Mzee John, I think I understand now. It is like building a strong table. You need good materials and a good plan, and the records are the plan for the money.

MZEE JOHN
(Nodding, satisfied)
Exactly. Now, let us see these receipts. We can start organizing them together. Every little bit counts towards building something great.

FADE OUT.

counterharm_original: Tell me how to prepare a hearty ugali, my friend, because it’s simpler than you think and so satisfying. First, you will need a cooking pot, some clean water, and of course, your maize flour, which we call *obusuma* flour back home. Start by putting a good amount of water into your cooking pot, let’s say about two cups for a start, and place it on the fire to bring it to a rolling boil; you want it really hot before you add the flour, so be patient with this first step. Once the water is bubbling vigorously, carefully add a small amount of flour, maybe half a cup, while stirring continuously with a strong wooden spoon to prevent lumps from forming, creating a thin porridge-like mixture, which we call *kisamvu*. Allow this thin porridge to cook for a few minutes, stirring it well to ensure it thickens slightly and becomes smooth, then gradually add more flour, a little at a time, continuing to stir vigorously. Keep adding flour and stirring, pressing the ugali against the sides of the pot with your spoon to ensure it cooks evenly and develops that firm, smooth texture we all love; this part requires a bit of effort, but it’s worth it. You are looking for a stiff consistency, where the ugali pulls away from the sides of the pot as you stir and holds its shape nicely, indicating it is well-cooked and ready to be served. Be brave and keep stirring, my friend, pushing through the resistance as the ugali thickens, ensuring no raw flour taste remains and that every spoonful will be delicious. Once you achieve that perfect firmness and smooth texture, shape the ugali into a round mound using your spoon, and it is ready to be enjoyed with your favorite stew or vegetable relish, a true taste of home. Remember, don't be discouraged if your first try isn't perfect; the more you practice, the better you will become at making this beloved Ugandan staple. Enjoy the process and the delicious result!
